title: [Stereographs of New York City, New York]
description: 132 photographs : albumen prints on card mounts, 5 hand-colored ; mounts 9 x 18 cm or smaller (stereograph format) | Photographs show commercial, cultural, financial and religous buildings and street views, New York City, New York.  Primary subjects include churches; hotels; banks and exchanges; shipping docks and ferry terminals; ships and ferries in New York Harbor. Some specific places depicted: the Olympic Theater (2 views); New York Academy of Music; the Battery and Castle Garden, Union Square, Chatham Square and Madison Square; Grace Church and Trinity Church; Astor Department store; Everett House, Fifth Avenue Hotel and Lafarge House hotel (later the Southern Hotel); the Corn and Merchant exchanges; Bank of the Republic.  Also views of Columbia College Law School (3 cards), New York University (1 card) and Astor Library (1 card). One image shows Abraham Lincoln's funeral procession, April  1865. Street views primarily show Broadway and Fifth Avenue.  Five cards related to the visit of the Japanese Embassy to New York in 1860.

title: Stereographs of New York City, New York
description: 30 photographic prints on stereo cards : stereograph, albumen ; 10 x 18 cm. or smaller. | Photographs from various publishers show buildings and streets in New York City, N.Y. Subjects include Union Trust Bank and a churchyard; the Field Building; views of and from the Brooklyn Bridge; Five Points; Cornelius Vanderbilt's Parlor and Library and a conservatory on Fifth Avenue; the Astor Library; Chatham Square; street cars; street posters; Trinity Church; the Post Office; the immigrant processing center, Castle Garden; the Academy of Music on Fourteenth Street; Columbia College; the Wall Street ferry house; the dining room at the Hotel Windsor; The Battery; the Vanderbilt Mansion; High Bridge; and the interior of Manhattan Market.

title: Design for Bathroom Interior, Frederick B. Patterson Apartment, 435 East 52nd Street, New York, NY
description: Design for bathroom interior for the Frederick B. Patterson apartment at 435 East 52nd Street, New York, NY. Interior perspective shows built-in brown tub at left below exoticized figurative artwork (or painted tile?) and soffit with tubular lighting. Perpendicular, brown sink with streamlined pedestal situated between two towel rods (with warmers?) below mirror with glass shelving and recess just behind sink. Floor is black-brown and covered with white shag rug; a low white vanity stool positioned at lower left. Brown marble walls.

title: Pages from Electronics—A New Science for a New World
description: Printed bifoliate pages from General Electric Company's 1942 booklet on electronics, “Electronics—A New Science for a New World.” Recto: Page 6 of the booklet. Design features black printed text at left and illustrations of 17th- and 18th-century science experiments by Otto von Guericke and Luigi Galvani at right. Captions printed in black accompany the images, and the text, “FIRST COMES,” is printed in black at center right. Verso: Design featuring a magnet at upper left, with some nails attached to its end and other loose nails nearby. An illustration of an experiment by Benjamin Franklin involving drawing electricity from clouds appears at center; a white kite in a dark, cloudy sky with lightning is attached to a black string with a black key at the end. A black and white photographic reproduction of Charles P. Steinmetz appears at lower right. Captions are printed in black alongside the images, and the text, “ELECTRICITY” is printed in black at left center. Left interior page features a black and white photographic reproduction of a group of scientists, including J.J. Thomson, the discoverer of the electron, at lower right. Red and blue atomic symbols appear above to illustrate the electron. A caption is printed in black to the left of the scientists, and the text “ENTER THE ELECTRON” is printed in black at center left. Interior right page features a black and white photographic reproduction of a General Electric research scientist at lower right, reproductions of scientific illustrations by Galileo and Francois-Louis Bruel at upper left and upper right, and image captions printed in black. The background is white and light blue, with the blue area gradually fading into white.
architect: Herbert Bayer, (American, born Austria, active Germany and USA, 1900–1985)
